text:
Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are persistent "forever chemicals" that pose significant risks to human health and ecosystems even at ultratrace (ng·L⁻¹) concentrations. Despite the urgency of monitoring these pollutants across distributed water infrastructures, current detection methods rely on cumbersome, laboratory-bound equipment. There remains a critical need for portable technologies capable of on-site ultratrace analysis. In this work, we present a sensing platform based on amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs) designed for the selective detection of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) at parts-per-trillion levels.

These AFPs feature highly fluorinated poly($p$-phenylene ethynylene) and polyfluorene backbones integrated with pyridine-based selectors. The sensing mechanism utilizes a proton-transfer reaction between the acidic PFAS and the pyridine units. The fluorinated regions of the polymer drive the partitioning of PFAS from the aqueous phase into the polymer matrix, while the resulting protonated pyridinium sites act as low-energy exciton traps, causing a distinct red-shift in the fluorescence emission. We evaluated the performance of these AFPs in both thin-film and nanoparticle (CPdot) formats; the films achieved detection limits of approximately 1 ppb, while the increased surface area of the nanoparticles further enhanced sensitivity to roughly 100 ppt. Notably, the sensing response remained robust across various water matrices, showing consistent performance in MilliQ, deionized, and complex well water. These findings offer a high-sensitivity, scalable approach for the real-time monitoring of aqueous PFAS in the field.
